channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: For Artemis II, Returning to Earth May Be the Most Dangerous Part of the Mission
item.description: After a successful flight around the moon, the astronauts are relying on a flawed heat shield to protect them as they re-enter Earth’s atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: These Chimps Began the Bloodiest ‘War’ on Record. No One Knows Why.
item.description: A long-running conflict in a Ugandan park may provide clues to the origins of human warfare, and how to avoid it.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: NASA Prepares for Artemis II Splashdown After Historic Moon Flyby
item.description: The mission is seen as a key step toward resuming crewed lunar landings. Artemis II’s four astronauts are scheduled to land in the waters off Southern California on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Qasim Nauman
item.media:description: The Artemis II crew, from left, Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch, Victor Glover and Jeremy Hansen during a video conference on Wednesday.

item.title: See First Photos From NASA’s Artemis II Moon Mission Capturing a Setting Earth and Eclipse
item.description: The White House and NASA released imagery captured by the cameras of the four astronauts of the Artemis II mission.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: An Earthset, captured by the Artemis II crew from the Orion Integrity spacecraft during Monday’s flyby.

item.title: Artemis II Astronauts Head Home After Historic Journey Around the Moon
item.description: The NASA lunar flyby took the four crew members farther from Earth than any humans. They witnessed a solar eclipse and received praise in a call from President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang, Katrina Miller and Thomas Fuller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: NASA’s Lunar Science Team at Johnson Space Center in Houston watching the Artemis II flyby of the Moon.

item.title: NASA Families Don’t Go to the Moon, but They’re on the Mission, Too
item.description: For the families of the Artemis II astronauts, the mission “begins at assignment.”
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: Astronaut family members and others — hosted by Pat Collins, left, the astronaut Michael Collins’s wife — watching Apollo 11 television coverage on July 20, 1969.

item.title: E.P.A. Says It Will End Biden’s Coal Ash Disposal Rules
item.description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, said the changes would help U.S. “energy dominance.” Environmentalists said they threaten drinking water.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: The aftermath of a coal ash spill after a retention pond wall collapsed in Harriman, Tenn., in 2008.

item.title: New Charter Allows RFK Jr. to Reclaim Vaccine Policy Despite Court Ruling
item.description: The charter, published on Thursday, alters the makeup and purpose of the panel, opening the door for Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to reclaim his revision of national vaccine policy.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Aaron Siri, a lawyer aligned with Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., speaking at a meeting of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices in Atlanta in December.

item.title: Climate Change Denial Sees a Resurgence in Trump’s Washington
item.description: A conference near the White House drew hundreds of people who reject the scientific consensus on climate change. The mood was triumphant.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: William Happer, a Princeton professor emeritus of physics who has argued that global warming is good for humanity, during a presentation at the Heartland Institute’s International Climate Change Conference in Washington on Wednesday.

item.title: I.U.C.N. Red List Moves Emperor Penguins to “Endangered”
item.description: Populations are declining as climate change causes the sea ice the birds need for survival to retreat, according to researchers.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer
item.category: International Union for Conservation of Nature

item.title: Vegetative Patients May Be More Aware Than We Knew
item.description: New research is upending what we thought about the consciousness of patients, leaving families with agonizing choices.
item.dc:creator: Katie Engelhart

item.title: Why Manatees Need Humans to Slow Down and Pay Attention
item.description: These gentle giants forage in shallow waters, primarily along the coast of Florida, and often have fatal encounters with boats. What can be done to help them?
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.category: Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission
item.media:credit: Zack Wittman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Manatees in the Wakulla River in Florida in 2024. Collisions with boats accounted for 25 percent of their deaths in Florida last year, state figures show.

item.title: Patients Are Using Chatbots to Fight Medical Bills, With Mixed Results
item.description: While chatbots like Claude and ChatGPT can help narrow the information divide between patients and providers, they can also dispense flawed advice.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kwon
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jackie Davalos, right, and her partner, Walter Kerr, used a chatbot to get advice on how to challenge a $22,604 hospital bill.

item.title: Artemis II Astronauts Get a Break After Journey Around the Moon
item.description: The crew of the NASA mission had a quiet day as they flew home toward Earth.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: The Artemis II astronauts aboard their Orion spacecraft, left, observed the moon eclipsing the sun on Monday for about 53 minutes.

item.title: How Psychedelics Affect the Brain
item.description: An analysis of hundreds of images from several studies shows how hallucinogenic drugs drive activity in various regions of the brain.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Jacobs
item.media:description: A new study suggests that psychedelic compounds in substances like psilocybin or LSD temporarily reduce the separation between how we think and how we perceive.

item.title: NASA’s Artemis II Astronauts Spread ‘Moon Joy’ to the Public
item.description: While science can seem colorless and plain, NASA’s lunar crew members have brought expressiveness and emotion about their journey to mission control and the public.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: An Artemis II crew selfie midway through their lunar observation period. Clockwise from bottom, Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Jeremy Hansen and Christina Koch.

item.title: Idaho Cut Services for People With Schizophrenia. Then the Deaths Began.
item.description: Eliminating outreach to people with severe mental illness set off such a cascade of bad outcomes that Idaho has scrambled to reverse the cuts.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lorenzo Pahvitse-Rodriguez is survived by four siblings, including, from left, Marquis and Sequoia, and by his mother, Lori.

item.title: Trump Calls Artemis II Astronauts After Their Historic Journey Around the Moon
item.description: The conversation celebrated a small, but significant, step in an ambitious plan for missions to the moon and Mars that Mr. Trump had set early in his first term.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:description: Artemis II astronauts speaking with President Trump from their Orion spacecraft on Monday.

item.title: The hundreds of mementos aboard Artemis II include a flag that never made it to the moon.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Apollo Monument in Titusville, Fla., in January. The American flag that was selected to travel with the Apollo 18 mission, canceled by NASA in 1971, will travel with Artemis II on Wednesday.

item.title: The first earthlings to the moon weren’t humans. They were tortoises.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:description: Zond 5 after splashdown in the Indian Ocean; Soviet scientists examining the tortoises flown around the moon aboard Zond 5.

item.title: The crew will see a solar eclipse while on the far side of the moon.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Ashley L. Conti for The New York Times
item.media:description: The crew of Artemis II will have a chance to see a solar eclipse, but it may not look like the Great American Eclipse of April 2024.

item.title: The Dark Side of the Moon Is Really the Far Side
item.description: Scientists prefer to call the backside of the moon its “far” side.
item.dc:creator: Andrea Kannapell
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: The moon’s backside is lit by the sun, but out of reach of human vision from our planet’s surface.

item.title: As the astronauts lose contact with Earth, NASA isn’t worried.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: The four astronauts will face a radio blackout as they travel around the moon.

item.title: How to Follow NASA’s Artemis II Around the Far Side of the Moon
item.description: The astronauts will head into a communications blackout at 6:44 p.m. Eastern time as they become the first people to travel around the moon since 1972.
item.dc:creator: Anushka Patil
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: NASA’s Lunar Science Team monitoring a topographical map of the moon on Monday in the Science Evaluation Room at Johnson Space Center in Houston.

item.title: The Artemis II crew breaks a distance record.
item.description: Three Americans and one Canadian traveling on NASA’s Orion spacecraft surpassed a milestone set by the crew of Apollo 13 in 1970.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: Victor Glover peering out the Orion spacecraft’s windows, looking back at Earth ahead of the crew’s lunar flyby, on Monday.

item.title: Artemis II Astronauts Name Moon Crater After Carroll, Reid Wiseman’s Late Wife
item.description: The crew shared an emotional moment with mission control and the family of Reid Wiseman, whose wife, Carroll, died in 2020, on the ground in Houston.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: Reid and Carroll Wiseman. Ms. Wiseman died of cancer before the Artemis II mission.

item.title: Human eyes are seeing the far side of the moon for the first time in a long time.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Artemis II lunar science team discussing lunar observation strategies around a large touchscreen table in the Science Evaluation Room of the Johnson Space Center in Houston.

item.title: Exploring the Far Side of the Moon
item.description: As Artemis II flies around the far side of the moon, our graphics editor Marco Hernandez describes what the astronauts are looking for.
item.dc:creator: Marco Hernandez, Melanie Bencosme, Jon Miller, Gabriel Blanco, Joey Sendaydiego and Lauren Pruitt

item.title: This is the most important science experiment aboard Artemis II.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Cassandra Klos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Prince Saini, a scientist at Emulate Biotech in Boston, with AVATAR, A Virtual Astronaut Tissue Analog Response chip, which is traveling into space with samples of bone marrow from the crew.

item.title: Suspect in Hacking of Climate Activists Is Extradited to New York
item.description: Prosecutors say Amit Forlit ran a global hacking operation on behalf of a Washington lobbying group that aimed to thwart environmental lawsuits against oil companies.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: The clients of the lobbying firm that is alleged to have hired Amit Forlit included Exxon Mobil, according to a legal filing.

item.title: Here’s What Food the Artemis II Astronauts Will Eat in Space
item.description: The Artemis II astronauts have scheduled times for breakfast, lunch and dinner with set menus based on their personal preferences and nutritional needs.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Ahn
item.media:description: Various food pouches and a prototype of a mini potable water dispenser, a version of which is being used to rehydrate food during the mission.

item.title: Federal Agency Unveils Three Potential Osteoarthritis Treatments
item.description: With funding from ARPA-H, three teams of researchers have regrown bone and cartilage, even entire knees, in animal studies. Human trials are not far off.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.category: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health
item.media:description: A colored X-ray of a 66-year-old patient with osteoarthritis.

item.title: Trump’s USAID Overhaul Sent Millions More Dollars to Big U.S.-Based Contractors
item.description: While organizations in the developing world were nearly shut out, the big aid agencies DOGE had called wasteful received huge infusions of cash, a new analysis found.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Taiwo Aina for The New York Times
item.media:description: Women in Borno, Nigeria, waiting with their children to receive nutrition treatment and therapeutic food at an outpatient therapeutic program run by FHI 360, a nonprofit based in North Carolina.

item.title: Artemis II Astronauts Set New Distance Record in Moon Flyby: What to Know
item.description: The journey around the moon of three Americans and one Canadian is going into its sixth day, but it’s not too late to get caught up on it.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: The S.L.S. rocket taking off from Launch Complex 39B from NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida to begin the Artemis II mission last Wednesday.

item.title: NASA Artemis II Astronauts Race Into Moon’s Embrace After Quiet Easter
item.description: Ahead of a lunar flyby on Monday, the crew celebrated the astronaut Jeremy Hansen’s first spaceflight and got a special message from Charlie Duke, the Apollo 16 moonwalker.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: A view of the near side of the moon, the side we always see from Earth, captured by the Artemis II astronauts on Sunday.

item.title: Where Are NASA’s Artemis II Astronauts Now? Closer to the Moon Than Earth.
item.description: The astronauts said they had lost track of which day it is on Earth on their transit to the moon.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: In a photo taken by a crew member, the moon is seen from a window on the Orion spacecraft Integrity during the Artemis II mission on Friday.

item.title: How Scientifically Accurate Is ‘Project Hail Mary’? Experts Weigh In
item.description: Based on hard science fiction, a genre that prioritizes scientific accuracy, the blockbuster gets a lot right but misses a few things, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: For Many Patients Leaving the I.C.U., the Struggle Has Only Just Begun
item.description: A long stay in intensive care can bring physical, cognitive and mental health challenges that can take months or longer to resolve.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span

item.title: Houston Cheers on Artemis II Moon Mission, Reclaiming Its Place as ‘Space City’
item.description: The Artemis II mission elicited deep feelings for many Americans, particularly in Houston, the home of mission control.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Meridith Kohut
item.media:description: Over a thousand people gathered at Space Center Houston to watch the launch of the Artemis II rocket.

item.title: Forest Service Will Close Research Stations That Study Wildfire Risk
item.description: Scientists say their work on fires and climate change could be lost as the agency moves its headquarters to Utah from Washington and shuts 57 research stations.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: John Stember for The New York Times

item.title: NASA’s Artemis II Astronauts Took iPhones Into Space
item.description: The astronauts traveling in the Artemis II spacecraft were allowed to take smartphones with them. Sadly, they can’t connect to the internet.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang

item.title: H.H.S. Takes a First Step Toward Restoring Vaccine Advisory Committee
item.description: A federal judge last month questioned the legitimacy of the panel and overturned its recent work. A notice suggests that it may be revived on Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s terms.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:description: The membership and decisions of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, which recommends vaccines for Americans, were halted last month by a federal judge.

item.title: Artemis II Pilot Test Drove the Orion Capsule on the Way to the Moon
item.description: Victor Glover, a former Navy test pilot, carefully maneuvered the Orion capsule in space around a discarded rocket stage. The demonstration is crucial for future moon landing missions.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: NASA Unveils 1st Earth Photos From Artemis II Moon Mission: ‘You Look Beautiful.’
item.description: The pictures were released on the third day of the first mission since 1972 to send people around the moon.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
